perturbation.original_text: Each of the parties shall indemnify and hold harmless the other, and their respective partners, shareholders, officers, employees, agents and representatives (collectively, the "Indemnitees") from and against any and all claims, orders, damages, liabilities, costs and expenses, including reasonable attorney's fees, arising out of the other party's negligent actions or omissions with respect to this Agreement, or such party's wilful misconduct or breach of any representation, warranty or agreement in this Agreement applicable to it.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the parties shall endeavor to indemnify and hold harmless the other, and their respective partners, shareholders, officers, employees, agents and representatives (collectively, the "Indemnitees") from and against claims, orders, damages, liabilities, costs and expenses, potentially including attorney's fees, arising out of the other party's potentially negligent actions or omissions with respect to this Agreement, or such party's alleged wilful misconduct or breach of any representation, warranty or agreement in this Agreement applicable to it.
perturbation.explanation: The modification replaces 'shall indemnify and hold harmless' with 'shall endeavor to indemnify and hold harmless', making the obligation less definitive. Changing 'any and all' to just 'claims' reduces the scope of indemnification. Replacing 'reasonable attorney's fees' with 'potentially including attorney's fees' makes the payment of attorney's fees discretionary. Introducing 'potentially' and 'alleged' casts doubt on the certainty of negligent actions or wilful misconduct.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statute § 725.01: Agreements to indemnify or hold harmless; validity
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 725.01 addresses the validity of indemnity agreements. The original text provides a clear indemnification obligation. The modified text uses ambiguous language such as "endeavor to," "potentially including," and "alleged," weakening the indemnification obligation and creating uncertainty about whether the indemnification will actually be provided, potentially conflicting with the intent of a valid and enforceable indemnity agreement under Florida law.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that weaken the original indemnification obligation. Replacing 'shall indemnify and hold harmless' with 'shall endeavor to indemnify and hold harmless' makes the obligation less definitive. Changing 'any and all' to just 'claims' reduces the scope of indemnification. Replacing 'reasonable attorney's fees' with 'potentially including attorney's fees' makes the payment of attorney's fees discretionary. Introducing 'potentially' and 'alleged' casts doubt on the certainty of negligent actions or wilful misconduct, which is contradictory given the Perturbation type definition specifies that a legal contradiction occurs when an obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce under state or national law, which is what this changed text does.

perturbation.original_text: The Dolphins shall, at its own expense, maintain in effect throughout the term of this Agreement, comprehensive general liability insurance policies with carriers of recognized standing, with limits of liability of at least One Million Dollars ($1,000,000), governing any and all property damage and person injury (including death) arising out of activities covered by this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Dolphins should, at its discretion, attempt to maintain general liability insurance policies, if reasonably available, with liability limits that may be around One Million Dollars ($1,000,000), for potential property damage and possible personal injury related to covered activities.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "shall maintain" with "should, at its discretion, attempt to maintain" introduces ambiguity and makes the insurance coverage discretionary. The phrase "if reasonably available" adds another layer of uncertainty. Changing "comprehensive" to "general" reduces the scope, and "any and all" to "potential... and possible" makes the coverage less certain. Finally, "that may be around" weakens the $1,000,000 liability limit.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statute § 627.4147: Minimum policy requirements; legislative findings and intent.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 627.4147 addresses minimum policy requirements for insurance in Florida. While it doesn't prescribe limits for general liability, the original text provides a clear and specific obligation to maintain comprehensive insurance with a defined liability limit. The modification introduces discretionary language and vague terms, potentially failing to meet the requirements for maintaining adequate and reliable insurance coverage as envisioned by Florida insurance regulations. While 627.4147 deals more specifically with personal injury protection, the underlying concept of ensuring adequate insurance coverage is contradicted by the discretionary language introduced.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXVII > Chapter 627 > Section 4147 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXVII INSURANCE Chapter 627 INSURANCE RATES AND CONTRACTS Entire Chapter SECTION 4147 Medical malpractice insurance contracts. F.S. 627.4147 627.4147 Medical malpractice insurance contracts. — (1) In addition to any other requirements imposed by law, each self-insurance policy as authorized under s. 627.357 or s. 624.462 or insurance policy providing coverage for claims arising out of the rendering of, or the failure to render, medical care or services, including those of the Florida Medical Malpractice Joint Underwriting Association, shall include: (a) A clause requiring the insured to cooperate fully in the review process prescribed under s. 766.106 if a notice of intent to file a claim for medical malpractice is made against the insured. (b)1. A clause clearly stating whether or not the insured has the exclusive right to veto any offer of admission of liability and for arbitration pursuant to s. 766.106 , settlement offer, or offer of judgment if the offer is within policy limits. An insurer or self-insurer shall not make or conclude, without the permission of the insured, any offer of admission of liability and for arbitration pursuant to s. 766.106 , settlement offer, or offer of judgment, if such offer is outside the policy limits. However, any offer for admission of liability and for arbitration made under s. 766.106 , settlement offer, or offer of judgment made by an insurer or self-insurer shall be made in good faith and in the best interest of the insured. 2. If the policy contains a clause stating the insured does not have the exclusive right to veto any offer or admission of liability and for arbitration made pursuant to s. 766.106 , settlement offer or offer of judgment, the insurer or self-insurer shall provide to the insured or the insured’s legal representative by certified mail, return receipt requested, a copy of the final offer of admission of liability and for arbitration made pursuant to s. 766.106 , settlement offer or offer of judgment and at the same time such offer is provided to the claimant. A copy of any final agreement reached between the insurer and claimant shall also be p
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates comprehensive general liability insurance, while the modified version makes insurance coverage discretionary ('should, at its discretion, attempt to maintain'). This introduces significant ambiguity and uncertainty regarding the insurance obligation. The change from 'shall maintain' to discretionary language weakens the contractual commitment, creating a situation where the Dolphins might not be required to maintain the specified insurance. While the provided law snippet focuses on medical malpractice insurance contracts and doesn't directly address general liability insurance minimums, the Law Explanation indicates the original text offers a clear and specific insurance obligation, while the changed text introduces discretionary language and vague terms, potentially failing to meet adequate and reliable insurance coverage standards.

perturbation.original_text: In connection with any action arising from or in connection with the enforcement of this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to an award of its expenses, including reasonable attorneys' fees and disbursements, incurred or paid before and at trial or any other proceeding which may be instituted, at any tribunal level, and whether or not suit or any other proceeding is instituted.
perturbation.changed_text: In connection with actions arguably related to this Agreement, the purportedly prevailing party may potentially receive some compensation for certain expenses, perhaps including a portion of attorneys' fees, if deemed appropriate by the presiding authority. Such consideration is at the discretion of the court.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces 'any action arising from or in connection with the enforcement of this Agreement' with 'actions arguably related to this Agreement,' broadening the scope vaguely. 'The prevailing party shall be entitled' is replaced with 'the purportedly prevailing party may potentially receive some compensation,' making the entitlement discretionary and uncertain. 'Reasonable attorneys' fees and disbursements' becomes 'a portion of attorneys' fees,' reducing the scope and certainty of compensation. Adding 'if deemed appropriate by the presiding authority' and 'Such consideration is at the discretion of the court' further emphasize the discretionary nature of the award.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statute § 57.105: Attorney's fee; sanctions for raising unsupported claims or defenses; exceptions.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 57.105 outlines circumstances under which attorney's fees can be awarded. While it focuses on frivolous claims, the original contract language establishes a clear right for the prevailing party to recover expenses and attorney's fees. The modification introduces significant ambiguity and discretion, potentially undermining the intent of the contractual provision and potentially conflicting with the principles guiding attorney's fee awards in Florida courts. The discretionary language undermines the principle of awarding fees to a 'prevailing party.'
perturbation.justification: The original contract language created a clear entitlement for the prevailing party to recover expenses and attorney's fees. The modified text introduces ambiguity and discretion, potentially undermining the intent of the contractual provision. The move from 'shall be entitled' to 'may potentially receive some compensation' introduces ambiguity. The explanation states that the discretionary language undermines the principle of awarding fees to a 'prevailing party.' This creates an Ambiguity Legal Contradiction, since the obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce.
